title: Loop Termination Conditions
concept: Detecting goal completion, error thresholds, max steps limits, or cost ceilings to stop the agent cleanly.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: why does an agent keep 'state' between loop steps?

options.text: So it remembers its plan and past tool results instead of starting over
So it can run faster on a smaller computer
So the user never has to type anything

exercises.explain: State persistence is the agent's memory. It keeps the plan and earlier tool results across loop iterations so the agent builds on progress instead of losing it and restarting.

cards.title: When does it stop?
cards.body: An agent loops: think, act, check, repeat. But a loop with no exit could run forever. Something must tell it 'you're done, stop now.'

cards.title: Termination conditions
cards.body: These are the rules that end an agent's loop cleanly. Common ones: the goal is finished, too many errors happened, too many steps ran, or the cost got too high.

cards.title: Like a pizza delivery driver
cards.body: A driver stops when the pizza is delivered (goal done), or if the car breaks down (too many errors), or if they've driven their max hours (step limit), or if gas money runs out (cost ceiling).

cards.title: A research agent
cards.body: It searches, reads, and writes an answer. It stops the moment the answer is complete. If it fails 5 searches in a row, it also stops so it doesn't waste time forever.

cards.title: Myth: agents just know when to quit
cards.body: Not true. Without clear stop rules, an agent can loop endlessly or burn huge cost. Developers must set the exit conditions on purpose.

cards.title: Clean stop vs runaway loop
cards.body: Good termination protects your time and money. No termination is dangerous.

left.text: Agent finishes the goal or halts safely at a limit

right.text: Agent loops forever, racking up errors and cost

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a 'cost ceiling' termination condition?

options.text: Stop once total spending on tools or tokens reaches a set limit
Stop after the agent greets the user
Stop when the internet is fast
Stop when the user's name is short

exercises.explain: A cost ceiling caps how much money or tokens the agent may use. When spending hits that cap, the loop ends so costs don't spiral out of control.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A 'max steps' limit stops the agent after a set number of loop iterations, even if the goal isn't done.
exercises.explain: True. A max steps limit is a safety net. It caps how many times the loop can run so a stuck agent can't cycle forever.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about detecting success.
exercises.sentence: The cleanest way to end a loop is detecting that the goal is complete.
exercises.explain: The ideal stop happens when the goal is complete, meaning the agent actually achieved what it was asked to do. Other conditions like errors or cost are safety fallbacks.

exercises.prompt: Which stopping message sounds like it was written by an AI agent hitting a termination condition?

options.text: Stopping: reached the maximum of 10 steps without confirming the goal was met.
im tired lol im gonna stop now bye
The pizza was really good yesterday.

exercises.explain: Option A reads like a real termination log: it names the exact condition (max steps reached) and why it stopped. The others are casual or off-topic human chatter.

exercises.prompt: An agent is fixing a bug. It's tried 8 different fixes, all failed, and has spent nearly its whole budget. Which combination of termination conditions SHOULD end this loop, and why?

options.text: Error threshold and cost ceiling, because repeated failures plus near-max spending mean it should stop cleanly before wasting more
Only goal completion, because the agent must keep trying until the bug is fixed no matter what
None, because agents should never be stopped early
Only a max steps limit, and cost should be ignored entirely

exercises.explain: When an agent repeatedly fails (error threshold) AND is running out of budget (cost ceiling), those conditions together should trigger a clean stop. Good termination isn't only about success; error, step, and cost limits protect you when the goal can't be reached. Together they keep the agentic loop safe, affordable, and finite.
